title: Chapter 4 - Servers
description: Check your understanding of what a server is and what it does.

options.label: A server is always a laptop.
A server is the same thing as a URL.
A server is a computer (and software) that listens for requests and responds.
A server is a CSS file that styles the page.

explanations.overview: Servers are computers and programs that answer requests over a network.

byOptionId.a: Servers can be many things: a big machine, a small device, or a virtual machine in the cloud.
byOptionId.c: Correct. The key idea is listening and responding over a network.
byOptionId.d: CSS is for styling, not serving network requests.
byOptionId.b: A URL is an address, not the machine answering.

questions.prompt: Which task is a web server commonly responsible for?

options.label: Turning domain names into IP addresses
Serving static files like HTML and CSS
Drawing buttons on your screen directly

explanations.overview: Web servers often serve files and route requests to backend apps.

byOptionId.b: Correct. Serving static assets is a classic web server job.
byOptionId.c: That’s an operating system setting, not a server task.
byOptionId.d: The browser draws the UI, not the server.

questions.prompt: What is a backend app usually used for?

options.label: Implementing logic like login and returning API data

explanations.overview: Backend apps contain the rules and logic of a site or API, often talking to databases.

byOptionId.a: CSS selectors are front-end styling, not backend logic.
byOptionId.c: Correct. Backends handle logic and data processing.
byOptionId.d: Browsers are client programs; backends don’t replace them.
byOptionId.b: Cables aren’t controlled by your backend app.

questions.prompt: Which component commonly handles TLS termination and serves static files?

options.label: A reverse proxy / web server (e.g., Nginx)

explanations.overview: Reverse proxies or web servers often handle TLS and static asset delivery before handing requests to backend apps.

byOptionId.a: Correct — Nginx/Apache often terminate TLS and serve static content.
byOptionId.b: CSS preprocessors transform stylesheets but don’t handle TLS.
byOptionId.c: Keyboards are input devices, not servers.
byOptionId.d: DNS resolvers map names to IPs; they don’t terminate TLS for web traffic.
